title: Cold Comfort
passage:
When an agricultural station near the valley of Corrend began texting overnight frost warnings to local fruit growers, the assumption was plain: better forecasts would mean fewer ruined crops. The forecasts proved good. The losses barely moved. The researcher Nils Faber, who set two seasons of warnings against the growers' own harvest records, found that most orchardists already knew a hard night was on its way — the radio and their own thermometers had told them — and that the text rarely changed what they did next. Lighting smudge pots or running wind machines through a single cold night cost money and cost sleep, and on a borderline forecast many growers simply gambled that the frost would pass them by. Faber is careful not to generalise from one valley. Still, he suspects the service had answered a question the growers were not really asking: they were seldom short of warning, only of a cheap and easy way to act on it.
— Elin Sarosy

questions.prompt: What is the main point the text makes about the Corrend frost-warning service?

options.text: That the accurate frost forecasts sharply cut the fruit growers' crop losses over the two seasons Faber studied.
That the service sharpened the forecasts but did little to cut losses, because the growers' real difficulty was acting on a warning, not receiving one.
That the warnings largely failed because they were too inaccurate to tell growers when a hard frost was genuinely coming.
That the growers disregarded the alerts because they mistrusted a warning sent by text rather than heard on the radio.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-004 (main idea / whole-text point), short text. STEM-LAW: the stem asks for the main point about the service but does not entail whether the limiting factor was the forecast or the response. WHY-KEY (B): the passage sets up 'better forecasts would mean fewer ruined crops', then reports 'The forecasts proved good. The losses barely moved', because growers 'already knew a hard night was on its way' and protective action 'cost money and cost sleep', so 'on a borderline forecast many ... gambled'; Faber's closing point is that they were 'seldom short of warning, only of a cheap and easy way to act on it'. B captures that whole-text argument — good forecasts, little loss reduction, response as the true bottleneck. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = the intuitive expected result the text explicitly overturns — 'The losses barely moved'; a passage-blind reader cannot rule it out, since better forecasts cutting losses is the natural hypothesis the piece was written to test and reject. C = a live but wrong mechanism — 'the warnings failed because they were inaccurate' is a perfectly ordinary way a warning service fails, but the text states 'The forecasts proved good', so the flaw is only detectable from the passage; it is not eliminable by form. D = a plausible behavioural explanation the text never gives — growers gambled because acting 'cost money and cost sleep', not because they mistrusted the text channel; a reader who has not seen that line could readily entertain channel-mistrust. All three distractors are on-topic, internally coherent claims about the service, each defeated only by content the passage supplies.

generator_meta.topic: agricultural frost-warning service field study: accurate overnight forecasts that barely reduced crop losses because the growers' bottleneck was the cost and effort of acting, not the warning itself

fictional_source.byline: Elin Sarosy (invented science journalist); research attributed to invented researcher Nils Faber

fictional_entities: the valley of Corrend and its agricultural station (invented)
Nils Faber, researcher (invented; all findings invented)
generator_meta.regeneration_note: Regenerates a batch-4 main-idea slot killed on the distractor-plausibility axis, where three distractors self-eliminated by form. Fix applied: every distractor here is a substantive, on-topic, internally coherent claim a passage-blind reader would treat as live. A = the natural hypothesis the study rejects (losses did fall); C = an ordinary failure mode of a warning service (inaccuracy); D = a plausible behavioural cause (mistrust of the text channel). None is an absurd overclaim, a circular consequence-as-cause, or an off-topic option, and none is absolutised while the key alone stays hedged — each is defeated ONLY by content the passage states ('forecasts proved good', 'losses barely moved', cost/effort as the real cause).
generator_meta.spelling_note: BrE held throughout ('generalise'); no AmE-only spellings present.
generator_meta.engineered_traps: expected-result reversal at A (better forecasts should cut losses; text says they barely did), wrong-mechanism at C (failure blamed on inaccuracy; text says forecasts were good), wrong-cause at D (mistrust of channel; text says cost and effort); key B is the two-sided whole-text point and, crucially, is not the sole measured option — A, C and D are each confident, specific claims, so 'pick the qualified answer' does not identify the key.
generator_meta.self_blind_solve: Solved from the passage alone: B is the only option reconciling 'forecasts proved good' with 'losses barely moved' and the closing 'seldom short of warning, only of a ... way to act on it'. A is contradicted by 'losses barely moved', C by 'the forecasts proved good', D by the stated cost/sleep cause of the gambling. Exactly one defensible answer, but all three distractors remain genuinely tempting to a reader who has not read the passage. All entities fictional; no famous-thesis anchoring.
